finis villette
chapter i
bretton
my godmother live in a handsome house in the clean and ancient town of bretton
her husband s family have be resident there for generation and bore indeed the name of their birthplace--bretton of bretton whether by coincidence or because some remote ancestor have be a personage of sufficient importance to leave his name to his neighbourhood i know not
when i be a girl i go to bretton about twice a year and well i like the visit
the house and its inmate specially suit i
the large peaceful room the well-arrange furniture the clear wide window the balcony outside look down on a fine antique street where sunday and holiday seem always to abide--so quiet be its atmosphere so clean its pavement--these thing please i well
one child in a household of grown people be usually make very much of and in a quiet way i be a good deal take notice of by mrs
bretton who have be leave a widow with one son before i know she her husband a physician have die while she be yet a young and handsome woman
she be not young as i remember she but she be still handsome tall well-make and though dark for an englishwoman yet wear always the clearness of health in her brunette cheek and its vivacity in a pair of fine cheerful black eye
people esteem it a grievous pity that she have not confer her complexion on her son whose eye be blue--though even in boyhood very pierce--and the colour of his long hair such as friend do not venture to specify except as the sun shine on it when they call it golden
he inherit the line of his mother s feature however also her good tooth her stature or the promise of her stature for he be not yet full- grown and what be well her health without flaw and her spirit of that tone and equality which be well than a fortune to the possessor
in the autumn of the year i be stay at bretton my godmother have come in person to claim i of the kinsfolk with whom be at that time fix my permanent residence
i believe she then plainly see event come whose very shadow i scarce guess yet of which the faint suspicion suffice to impart unsettled sadness and make i glad to change scene and society
time always flow smoothly for i at my godmother s side not with tumultuous swiftness but blandly like the gliding of a full river through a plain
my visit to she resemble the sojourn of christian and hopeful beside a certain pleasant stream with green tree on each bank and meadow beautify with lily all the year round the charm of variety there be not nor the excitement of incident but i like peace so well and seek stimulus so little that when the latter come i almost feel it a disturbance and wish rather it have still hold aloof
one day a letter be receive of which the content evidently cause mrs
bretton surprise and some concern
i think at first it be from home and tremble expect i know not what disastrous communication to i however no reference be make and the cloud seem to pass
the next day on my return from a long walk i find as i enter my bedroom an unexpected change
in addition to my own french bed in its shady recess appear in a corner a small crib drape with white and in addition to my mahogany chest of drawer i see a tiny rosewood chest
i stand still gaze and consider
of what be these thing the sign and token i ask
the answer be obvious
a second guest be come mrs
bretton expect other visitor on descend to dinner explanation ensue
a little girl i be tell would shortly be my companion the daughter of a friend and distant relation of the late dr
bretton s
this little girl it be add have recently lose her mother though indeed mrs
bretton ere long subjoin the loss be not so great as might at first appear
mrs
home home it seem be the name have be a very pretty but a giddy careless woman who have neglect her child and disappoint and dishearten her husband
so far from congenial have the union prove that separation at last ensue--separation by mutual consent not after any legal process
soon after this event the lady have over-exert herself at a ball catch cold take a fever and die after a very brief illness
her husband naturally a man of very sensitive feeling and shock inexpressibly by too sudden communication of the news could hardly it seem now be persuade but that some over-severity on his part--some deficiency in patience and indulgence--have contribute to hasten her end
he have brood over this idea till his spirit be seriously affect the medical man insist on travel be try as a remedy and meanwhile mrs
bretton have offer to take charge of his little girl
and i hope add my godmother in conclusion the child will not be like she mamma as silly and frivolous a little flirt as ever sensible man be weak enough to marry
for say she mr
home be a sensible man in his way though not very practical he be fond of science and live half his life in a laboratory try experiment--a thing his butterfly wife could neither comprehend nor endure and indeed confess my godmother i should not have like it myself in answer to a question of mine she far inform i that her late husband use to say mr
home have derive this scientific turn from a maternal uncle a french savant for he come it seem of mixed french and scottish origin and have connection now live in france of whom more than one write de before his name and call himself noble
that same evening at nine o clock a servant be despatch to meet the coach by which our little visitor be expect
mrs
bretton and i sit alone in the drawing-room wait she come john graham bretton be absent on a visit to one of his schoolfellow who live in the country
my godmother read the evening paper while she wait i sew
it be a wet night the rain lash the pane and the wind sound angry and restless
poor child say mrs
bretton from time to time
what weather for her journey
i wish she be safe here a little before ten the door-bell announce warren s return
no soon be the door open than i run down into the hall there lie a trunk and some band-box beside they stand a person like a nurse-girl and at the foot of the staircase be warren with a shawled bundle in his arm
be that the child i ask
yes miss i would have open the shawl and try to get a peep at the face but it be hastily turn from i to warren s shoulder
put i down please say a small voice when warren open the drawing-room door and take off this shawl continue the speaker extract with its minute hand the pin and with a sort of fastidious haste doff the clumsy wrapping
the creature which now appear make a deft attempt to fold the shawl but the drapery be much too heavy and large to be sustain or wield by those hand and arm
give it to harriet please be then the direction and she can put it away this say it turn and fix its eye on mrs
bretton
come here little dear say that lady
come and let i see if you be cold and damp come and let i warm you at the fire the child advance promptly
relieved of her wrapping she appear exceedingly tiny but be a neat completely-fashioned little figure light slight and straight
seat on my godmother s ample lap she look a mere doll her neck delicate as wax her head of silky curl increase i think the resemblance
mrs
bretton talk in little fond phrase as she chafe the child s hand arm and foot first she be consider with a wistful gaze but soon a smile answer she
mrs
bretton be not generally a caress woman even with her deeply-cherish son her manner be rarely sentimental often the reverse but when the small stranger smile at she she kiss it ask what be my little one s name missy but besides missy polly papa call she will polly be content to live with i not always but till papa come home
papa be go away she shake her head expressively
he will return to polly or send for she will he ma am
do you know he will i think so but harriet think not at least not for a long while
he be ill her eye fill
she draw her hand from mrs
bretton s and make a movement to leave her lap it be at first resist but she said-- please i wish to go i can sit on a stool she be allow to slip down from the knee and take a footstool she carry it to a corner where the shade be deep and there seat herself
mrs
bretton though a commanding and in grave matter even a peremptory woman be often passive in trifle she allow the child her way
she say to i take no notice at present but i do take notice i watch polly rest her small elbow on her small knee her head on her hand i observe she draw a square inch or two of pocket-handkerchief from the doll-pocket of her doll-skirt and then i hear her weep
other child in grief or pain cry aloud without shame or restraint but this be weep the tiny occasional sniff testify to her emotion
mrs
bretton do not hear it which be quite as well
ere long a voice issue from the corner demanded-- may the bell be ring for harriet i ring the nurse be summon and come
harriet i must be put to bed say her little mistress
you must ask where my bed be harriet signify that she have already make that inquiry
ask if you sleep with i harriet no missy say the nurse you be to share this young lady s room designate i
missy do not leave her seat but i see her eye seek i
after some minute silent scrutiny she emerge from her corner
i wish you ma am good night say she to mrs
bretton but she pass i mute
good-night polly i say
no need to say good-night since we sleep in the same chamber be the reply with which she vanish from the drawing-room
we hear harriet propose to carry she up-stair
no need be again her answer-- no need no need and her small step toil wearily up the staircase
on go to bed an hour afterwards i find she still wide awake
she have arrange her pillow so as to support her little person in a sitting posture her hand place one within the other rest quietly on the sheet with an old-fashioned calm most unchildlike
i abstain from speak to she for some time but just before extinguish the light i recommend she to lie down
by and by be the answer
but you will take cold missy she take some tiny article of raiment from the chair at her crib side and with it cover her shoulder
i suffer she to do as she please
listen awhile in the darkness i be aware that she still wept wept under restraint quietly and cautiously
on awake with daylight a trickling of water catch my ear
behold
there she be rise and mount on a stool near the washstand with pain and difficulty incline the ewer which she could not lift so as to pour its content into the basin
it be curious to watch she as she wash and dress so small busy and noiseless
evidently she be little accustomed to perform her own toilet and the button string hook and eye offer difficulty which she encounter with a perseverance good to witness
she fold her night-dress she smooth the drapery of her couch quite neatly withdraw into a corner where the sweep of the white curtain conceal she she become still
i half rise and advance my head to see how she be occupy
on her knee with her forehead bend on her hand i perceive that she be pray
her nurse tap at the door
she start up
i be dress harriet say she i have dress myself but i do not feel neat
make i neat why do you dress yourself missy hush
speak low harriet for fear of wake the girl mean i who now lie with my eye shut
i dress myself to learn against the time you leave i do you want i to go when you be cross i have many a time want you to go but not now
tie my sash straight make my hair smooth please your sash be straight enough
what a particular little body you be it must be tie again
please to tie it there then
when i be go you must get that young lady to dress you on no account why
she be a very nice young lady
i hope you mean to behave prettily to she missy and not show your air she shall dress i on no account comical little thing you be not pass the comb straight through my hair harriet the line will be crook ay you be ill to please
do that suit pretty well
where should i go now that i be dress i will take you into the breakfast-room come then they proceed to the door
she stop
oh
harriet i wish this be papa s house
i donot know these people be a good child missy i be good but i ache here put her hand to her heart and moaning while she reiterate papa
papa i rouse myself and start up to check this scene while it be yet within bound
say good-morning to the young lady dictate harriet
she say good-morning and then follow her nurse from the room
harriet temporarily leave that same day to go to her own friend who live in the neighbourhood
on descend i find paulina the child call herself polly but her full name be paulina mary seat at the breakfast-table by mrs
bretton s side a mug of milk stand before she a morsel of bread fill her hand which lie passive on the table-cloth she be not eat
how we shall conciliate this little creature say mrs
bretton to i i donot know she taste nothing and by her look she have not sleep i express my confidence in the effect of time and kindness
if she be to take a fancy to anybody in the house she would soon settle but not till then reply mrs
bretton
chapter ii
paulina
some day elapse and it appear she be not likely to take much of a fancy to anybody in the house
she be not exactly naughty or wilful she be far from disobedient but an object less conducive to comfort--to tranquillity even--than she present it be scarcely possible to have before one s eye
she mop no grown person could have perform that uncheere business well no furrowed face of adult exile long for europe at europe s antipode ever bear more legibly the sign of home sickness than do her infant visage
she seem grow old and unearthly
i lucy snowe plead guiltless of that curse an overheated and discursive imagination but whenever open a room-door i find she seat in a corner alone her head in her pigmy hand that room seem to i not inhabit but haunted
and again when of moonlight night on wake i behold her figure white and conspicuous in its night-dress kneel upright in bed and pray like some catholic or methodist enthusiast--some precocious fanatic or untimely saint--i scarcely know what thought i have but they run risk of be hardly more rational and healthy than that child s mind must have be
i seldom catch a word of her prayer for they be whisper low sometimes indeed they be not whisper at all but put up unuttered such rare sentence as reach my ear still bear the burden papa my dear papa this i perceive be a one-idea d nature betray that monomaniac tendency i have ever think the most unfortunate with which man or woman can be curse
what might have be the end of this fretting have it continue unchecked can only be conjecture it receive however a sudden turn
one afternoon mrs
bretton coax she from her usual station in a corner have lift she into the window-seat and by way of occupy her attention tell she to watch the passenger and count how many lady should go down the street in a give time
she have sit listlessly hardly look and not count when--my eye be fix on her--i witness in its iris and pupil a startling transfiguration
these sudden dangerous natures-- sensitive as they be call--offer many a curious spectacle to those whom a cool temperament have secure from participation in their angular vagary
the fixed and heavy gaze swum tremble then glitter in fire the small overcast brow clear the trivial and dejected feature light up the sad countenance vanish and in its place appear a sudden eagerness an intense expectancy
it be be her word
like a bird or a shaft or any other swift thing she be go from the room how she get the house-door open i cannot tell probably it might be ajar perhaps warren be in the way and obey her behest which would be impetuous enough
i--watch calmly from the window-- see she in her black frock and tiny braided apron to pinafore she have an antipathy dart half the length of the street and as i be on the point of turning and quietly announce to mrs
bretton that the child be run out mad and ought instantly to be pursue i see she catch up and rapt at once from my cool observation and from the wonder stare of the passenger
a gentleman have do this good turn and now cover she with his cloak advance to restore she to the house whence he have see her issue
i conclude he would leave she in a servant s charge and withdraw but he enter have tarry a little while below he come up-stair
his reception immediately explain that he be know to mrs
bretton
she recognise he she greet he and yet she be flutter surprised take unawares
her look and manner be even expostulatory and in reply to these rather than her word he say i could not help it madam i find it impossible to leave the country without see with my own eye how she settle but you will unsettle she i hope not
and how be papa s little polly this question he address to paulina as he sit down and place she gently on the ground before he
how be polly s papa be the reply as she lean on his knee and gaze up into his face
it be not a noisy not a wordy scene for that i be thankful but it be a scene of feel too brimful and which because the cup do not foam up high or furiously overflow only oppress one the more
on all occasion of vehement unrestrained expansion a sense of disdain or ridicule come to the weary spectator s relief whereas i have ever feel most burdensome that sort of sensibility which bend of its own will a giant slave under the sway of good sense
mr
home be a stern-featured--perhaps i should rather say a hard- feature man his forehead be knotty and his cheekbone be mark and prominent
the character of his face be quite scotch but there be feel in his eye and emotion in his now agitated countenance
his northern accent in speak harmonise with his physiognomy
he be at once proud-looking and homely-looking
he lay his hand on the child s uplifted head
she said-- kiss polly he kiss she
i wish she would utter some hysterical cry so that i might get relief and be at ease
she make wonderfully little noise she seem to have get what she wanted-- all she want and to be in a trance of content
neither in mien nor in feature be this creature like her sire and yet she be of his strain her mind have be fill from his as the cup from the flagon
indisputably mr
home own manly self-control however he might secretly feel on some matter
polly he say look down on his little girl go into the hall you will see papa s great-coat lying on a chair put your hand into the pocket you will find a pocket- handkerchief there bring it to i she obey go and return deftly and nimbly
he be talk to mrs
bretton when she come back and she wait with the handkerchief in her hand
it be a picture in its way to see she with her tiny stature and trim neat shape stand at his knee
see that he continue to talk apparently unconscious of her return she take his hand open the unresisting finger insinuate into they the handkerchief and close they upon it one by one
he still seem not to see or to feel she but by-and-by he lift she to his knee she nestle against he and though neither look at nor speak to the other for an hour follow i suppose both be satisfied
during tea the minute thing s movement and behaviour give as usual full occupation to the eye
first she direct warren as he place the chair
put papa s chair here and mine near it between papa and mrs
bretton i must hand his tea she take her own seat and beckon with her hand to her father
be near i as if we be at home papa and again as she intercept his cup in pass and would stir the sugar and put in the cream herself i always do it for you at home papa nobody could do it as well not even your own self throughout the meal she continue her attention rather absurd they be
the sugar-tong be too wide for one of her hand and she have to use both in wield they the weight of the silver cream-ewer the bread-and-butter plate the very cup and saucer task her insufficient strength and dexterity but she would lift this hand that and luckily contrive through it all to break nothing
candidly speak i think she a little busy-body but her father blind like other parent seem perfectly content to let she wait on he and even wonderfully soothe by her office
she be my comfort he could not help say to mrs

when i say child i use an inappropriate and undescriptive term--a term suggest any picture rather than that of the demure little person in a mourning frock and white chemisette that might just have fit a good-sized doll--perch now on a high chair beside a stand whereon be her toy work-box of white varnish wood and hold in her hand a shred of a handkerchief which she be profess to hem and at which she bore perseveringly with a needle that in her finger seem almost a skewer prick herself ever and anon mark the cambric with a track of minute red dot occasionally start when the perverse weapon--swerve from her control--inflict a deep stab than usual but still silent diligent absorb womanly
graham be at that time a handsome faithless-look youth of sixteen
i say faithless-look not because he be really of a very perfidious disposition but because the epithet strike i as proper to describe the fair celtic not saxon character of his good look his waved light auburn hair his supple symmetry his smile frequent and destitute neither of fascination nor of subtlety in no bad sense
a spoiled whimsical boy he be in those day

your slave john graham bretton she look at he he rise and bow quite gravely
she deliberately put down thimble scissor work descend with precaution from her perch and curtsy with unspeakable seriousness say how do you do i have the honour to be in fair health only in some measure fatigue with a hurried journey

i hope ma am the present residence my mother s house appear to you a convenient place of abode not par-tic-er-er-ly i want to go home a natural and laudable desire ma am but one which notwithstanding i shall do my good to oppose
i reckon on be able to get out of you a little of that precious commodity call amusement which mamma and mistress snowe there fail to yield i i shall have to go with papa soon i shall not stay long at your mother s yes yes you will stay with i i be sure

do you like i no why i think you queer my face ma am your face and all about you you have long red hair auburn hair if you please mamma call it auburn or golden and so do all her friend
but even with my long red hair and he wave his mane with a sort of triumph--tawny he himself well know that it be and he be proud of the leonine hue i cannot possibly be queerer than be your ladyship you call i queer certainly after a pause i think i shall go to bed a little thing like you ought to have be in bed many hour since but you probably sit up in the expectation of see i no indeed you certainly wish to enjoy the pleasure of my society

chapter iii
the playmate
mr
home stay two day

bretton s chat which be just of the proper sort for a man in his morbid mood--not over-sympathetic yet not too uncongenial sensible and even with a touch of the motherly--she be sufficiently his senior to be permit this touch

chapter iv
miss marchmont
on quit bretton which i do a few week after paulina s departure--little thinking then i be never again to visit it never more to tread its calm old street--i betook myself home have be absent six month
it will be conjecture that i be of course glad to return to the bosom of my kindre
well
the amiable conjecture do no harm and may therefore be safely leave uncontradicted
far from say nay indeed i will permit the reader to picture i for the next eight year as a bark slumber through halcyon weather in a harbour still as glass--the steersman stretch on the little deck his face up to heaven his eye close bury if you will in a long prayer
a great many woman and girl be suppose to pass their life something in that fashion why not i with the rest
picture i then idle bask plump and happy stretch on a cushion deck warm with constant sunshine rock by breeze indolently soft
however it cannot be conceal that in that case i must somehow have fall overboard or that there must have be wreck at last
i too well remember a time--a long time--of cold of danger of contention
to this hour when i have the nightmare it repeat the rush and saltness of briny wave in my throat and their icy pressure on my lung
i even know there be a storm and that not of one hour nor one day
for many day and night neither sun nor star appear we cast with our own hand the tackling out of the ship a heavy tempest lie on we all hope that we should be save be take away
in fine the ship be lose the crew perish
as far as i recollect i complain to no one about these trouble
indeed to whom could i complain
of mrs
bretton i have long lose sight
impediment raise by other have year ago come in the way of our intercourse and cut it off
besides time have bring change for she too the handsome property of which she be leave guardian for her son and which have be chiefly invest in some joint-stock undertaking have melt it be say to a fraction of its original amount
graham i learn from incidental rumour have adopt a profession both he and his mother be go from bretton and be understand to be now in london
thus there remain no possibility of dependence on other to myself alone could i look
i know not that i be of a self-reliant or active nature but self-reliance and exertion be force upon i by circumstance as they be upon thousand besides and when miss marchmont a maiden lady of our neighbourhood send for i i obey her behest in the hope that she might assign i some task i could undertake
miss marchmont be a woman of fortune and live in a handsome residence but she be a rheumatic cripple impotent foot and hand and have be so for twenty year
she always sit upstairs her drawing-room adjoin her bed-room
i have often hear of miss marchmont and of her peculiarity she have the character of be very eccentric but till now have never see she
i find she a furrow grey-haired woman grave with solitude stern with long affliction irritable also and perhaps exact
it seem that a maid or rather companion who have wait on she for some year be about to be marry and she hear of my bereaved lot have send for i with the idea that i might supply this person s place
she make the proposal to i after tea as she and i sit alone by her fireside
it will not be an easy life say she candidly for i require a good deal of attention and you will be much confine yet perhaps contrast with the existence you have lately lead it may appear tolerable i reflect
of course it ought to appear tolerable i argue inwardly but somehow by some strange fatality it would not
to live here in this close room the watcher of suffering--sometimes perhaps the butt of temper--through all that be to come of my youth while all that be go have pass to say the least not blissfully
my heart sink one moment then it revive for though i force myself to realise evil i think i be too prosaic to idealise and consequently to exaggerate they
my doubt be whether i should have strength for the undertaking i observe
that be my own scruple say she for you look a wear-out creature so i do
i see myself in the glass in my mourning-dress a fade hollow-eyed vision
yet i think little of the wan spectacle
the blight i believe be chiefly external i still feel life at life s source
what else have you in view--anything nothing clear as yet but i may find something so you imagine perhaps you be right
try your own method then and if it do not succeed test mine
the chance i have offer shall be leave open to you for three month this be kind
i tell she so and express my gratitude
while i be speak a paroxysm of pain come on
i minister to she make the necessary application accord to her direction and by the time she be relieve a sort of intimacy be already form between we
i for my part have learn from the manner in which she bear this attack that she be a firm patient woman patient under physical pain though sometimes perhaps excitable under long mental canker and she from the good-will with which i succour she discover that she could influence my sympathy such as they be
she send for i the next day for five or six successive day she claim my company
close acquaintance while it develop both fault and eccentricity open at the same time a view of a character i could respect
stern and even morose as she sometimes be i could wait on she and sit beside she with that calm which always bless we when we be sensible that our manner presence contact please and soothe the person we serve
even when she scold i--which she do now and then very tartly--it be in such a way as do not humiliate and leave no sting it be rather like an irascible mother rat her daughter than a harsh mistress lecture a dependant lecture indeed she could not though she could occasionally storm
moreover a vein of reason ever run through her passion she be logical even when fierce
ere long a grow sense of attachment begin to present the thought of stay with she as companion in quite a new light in another week i have agree to remain
two hot close room thus become my world and a crippled old woman my mistress my friend my all
her service be my duty--her pain my suffering--her relief my hope--her anger my punishment--her regard my reward
i forget that there be field wood river sea an ever-change sky outside the steam-dim lattice of this sick chamber i be almost content to forget it
all within i become narrow to my lot
tame and still by habit discipline by destiny i demand no walk in the fresh air my appetite need no more than the tiny mess serve for the invalid
in addition she give i the originality of her character to study the steadiness of her virtue i will add the power of her passion to admire the truth of her feeling to trust
all these thing she have and for these thing i clung to she
for these thing i would have crawl on with she for twenty year if for twenty year long her life of endurance have be protract
but another decree be write
it seem i must be stimulate into action
i must be goad drive stung force to energy
my little morsel of human affection which i prize as if it be a solid pearl must melt in my finger and slip thence like a dissolve hailstone
my small adopt duty must be snatch from my easily content conscience
i have want to compromise with fate to escape occasional great agony by submit to a whole life of privation and small pain
fate would not so be pacify nor would providence sanction this shrink sloth and cowardly indolence
one february night--i remember it well--there come a voice near miss marchmont s house hear by every inmate but translate perhaps only by one
after a calm winter storm be usher in the spring
i have put miss marchmont to bed i sit at the fireside sewing
the wind be wail at the window it have wail all day but as night deepen it take a new tone--an accent keen pierce almost articulate to the ear a plaint piteous and disconsolate to the nerve trill in every gust
oh hush
hush i say in my disturbed mind drop my work and make a vain effort to stop my ear against that subtle search cry
i have hear that very voice ere this and compulsory observation have force on i a theory as to what it bode
three time in the course of my life event have teach i that these strange accent in the storm--this restless hopeless cry--denote a come state of the atmosphere unpropitious to life
epidemic disease i believe be often herald by a gasp sobbing torment long-lament east wind
hence i infer arise the legend of the banshee
i fancy too i have notice--but be not philosopher enough to know whether there be any connection between the circumstance--that we often at the same time hear of disturb volcanic action in distant part of the world of river suddenly rush above their bank and of strange high tide flow furiously in on low sea-coast
our globe i have say to myself seem at such period tear and disorder the feeble amongst we wither in her distempered breath rush hot from steam volcano i listen and tremble miss marchmont sleep
about midnight the storm in one half-hour fall to a dead calm
the fire which have be burn dead glow up vividly
i feel the air change and become keen
raise blind and curtain i look out and see in the star the keen sparkle of a sharp frost
turn away the object that meet my eye be miss marchmont awake lift her head from the pillow and regard i with unusual earnestness
be it a fine night she ask
i reply in the affirmative
i think so she say for i feel so strong so well
raise i
i feel young to-night she continue young light-hearte and happy
what if my complaint be about to take a turn and i be yet destine to enjoy health
it would be a miracle and these be not the day of miracle i think to myself and wonder to hear her talk so
she go on direct her conversation to the past and seem to recall its incident scene and personage with singular vividness i love memory to-night she say i prize she as my good friend
she be just now give i a deep delight she be bring back to my heart in warm and beautiful life reality--not mere empty idea but what be once reality and that i long have think decay dissolve mix in with grave-mould

i be not sick till long after we pass margate and deep be the pleasure i drink in with the sea-breeze divine the delight i draw from the heave channel wave from the sea-bird on their ridge from the white sail on their dark distance from the quiet yet becloud sky overhang all
in my reverie methought i see the continent of europe like a wide dream-land far away
sunshine lie on it make the long coast one line of gold tiny tracery of cluster town and snow-gleam tower of wood deep mass of height serrate of smooth pasturage and veiny stream emboss the metal-bright prospect
for background spread a sky solemn and dark blue and--grand with imperial promise soft with tint of enchantment--strode from north to south a god-bent bow an arch of hope
cancel the whole of that if you please reader--or rather let it stand and draw thence a moral--an alliterative text-hand copy-- day-dream be delusion of the demon
become excessively sick i falter down into the cabin

nothing could exceed her impatience and fretfulness

many a time since have i notice in person of ginevra fanshawe s light careless temperament and fair fragile style of beauty an entire incapacity to endure they seem to sour in adversity like small beer in thunder
the man who take such a woman for his wife ought to be prepared to guarantee she an existence all sunshine
indignant at last with her teasing peevishness i curtly request she to hold her tongue the rebuff do she good and it be observable that she like i no bad for it
as dark night draw on the sea roughen large wave sway strong against the vessel s side

article of furniture begin to fall about and it become needful to lash they to their place the passenger grow sicker than ever miss fanshawe declare with groan that she must die
not just yet honey say the stewardess
webe just in port accordingly in another quarter of an hour a calm fall upon we all and about midnight the voyage end
i be sorry yes i be sorry
my resting-time be past my difficulty--my stringent difficulty--recommence

yet where should i go
i must go somewhere
necessity dare not be nice

i follow this man along a rudely-pave street light now by a fitful gleam of moonlight he bring i to the inn

i give he a sovereign to change

how deeply glad i be when the door of a very small chamber at length close on i and my exhaustion
again i might rest though the cloud of doubt would be as thick to-morrow as ever the necessity for exertion more urgent the peril of destitution nearer the conflict for existence more severe

chapter viii
madame beck

she have a respect for angleterre and as to les anglaises she would have the woman of no other country about her own child if she could help it
often in the evening after she have be plot and counter- plotting spy and receive the report of spy all day she would come up to my room--a trace of real weariness on her brow--and she would sit down and listen while the child say their little prayer to i in english the lord s prayer and the hymn begin gentle jesus these little catholics be permit to repeat at my knee and when i have put they to bed she would talk to i i soon gain enough french to be able to understand and even answer she about england and englishwomen and the reason for what she be pleased to term their superior intelligence and more real and reliable probity
very good sense she often show very sound opinion she often broach she seem to know that keep girl in distrustful restraint in blind ignorance and under a surveillance that leave they no moment and no corner for retirement be not the good way to make they grow up honest and modest woman but she aver that ruinous consequence would ensue if any other method be try with continental child they be so accustomed to restraint that relaxation however guard would be misunderstood and fatally presume on
she be sick she would declare of the mean she have to use but use they she must and after discoursing often with dignity and delicacy to i she would move away on her soulier de silence and glide ghost-like through the house watch and spy everywhere peer through every keyhole listen behind every door
after all madame s system be not bad--let i do her justice
nothing could be well than all her arrangement for the physical well-being of her scholar
no mind be overtaske the lesson be well distribute and make incomparably easy to the learner there be a liberty of amusement and a provision for exercise which keep the girl healthy the food be abundant and good neither pale nor puny face be anywhere to be see in the rue fossette
she never grudge a holiday she allow plenty of time for sleep dressing washing eating her method in all these matter be easy liberal salutary and rational many an austere english school-mistress would do vastly well to imitate she--and i believe many would be glad to do so if exact english parent would let they
as madame beck rule by espionage she of course have her staff of spy she perfectly know the quality of the tool she use and while she would not scruple to handle the dirty for a dirty occasion-- fling this sort from she like refuse rind after the orange have be duly squeeze--i have know she fastidious in seek pure metal for clean us and when once a bloodless and rustless instrument be find she be careful of the prize keep it in silk and cotton- wool
yet woe be to that man or woman who rely on her one inch beyond the point where it be her interest to be trustworthy interest be the master-key of madame s nature--the mainspring of her motives-- the alpha and omega of her life
i have see she feeling appeal to and i have smile in half-pity half-scorn at the appellant
none ever gain her ear through that channel or sway her purpose by that mean
on the contrary to attempt to touch her heart be the sure way to rouse she antipathy and to make of her a secret foe
it prove to she that she have no heart to be touch it remind she where she be impotent and dead
never be the distinction between charity and mercy well exemplified than in she
while devoid of sympathy she have a sufficiency of rational benevolence she would give in the readiest manner to people she have never see--rather however to class than to individual
pour les pauvre she open her purse freely--against the poor man as a rule she keep it close
in philanthropic scheme for the benefit of society at large she take a cheerful part no private sorrow touch she no force or mass of suffering concentrate in one heart have power to pierce her
not the agony in gethsemane not the death on calvary could have wring from her eye one tear
i say again madame be a very great and a very capable woman
that school offer she for her power too limit a sphere she ought to have sway a nation she should have be the leader of a turbulent legislative assembly
nobody could have browbeat she none irritate her nerve exhaust her patience or over-reach her astuteness
in her own single person she could have comprise the duty of a first minister and a superintendent of police
wise firm faithless secret crafty passionless watchful and inscrutable acute and insensate--withal perfectly decorous--what more could be desire
the sensible reader will not suppose that i gain all the knowledge here condense for his benefit in one month or in one half-year
no
what i see at first be the thrive outside of a large and flourish educational establishment
here be a great house full of healthy lively girl all well-dress and many of they handsome gain knowledge by a marvellously easy method without painful exertion or useless waste of spirit not perhaps make very rapid progress in anything take it easy but still always employ and never oppress
here be a corps of teacher and master more stringently task as all the real head-labour be to be do by they in order to save the pupil yet have their duty so arrange that they relieve each other in quick succession whenever the work be severe here in short be a foreign school of which the life movement and variety make it a complete and most charming contrast to many english institution of the same kind
behind the house be a large garden and in summer the pupil almost live out of door amongst the rose-bush and the fruit-tree
under the vast and vine-drape berceau madame would take her seat on summer afternoon and send for the class in turn to sit round she and sew and read
meantime master come and go deliver short and lively lecture rather than lesson and the pupil make note of their instruction or do not make they--just as inclination prompt secure that in case of neglect they could copy the note of their companion
besides the regular monthly jour de sortie the catholic fete-day bring a succession of holiday all the year round and sometimes on a bright summer morning or soft summer evening the boarder be take out for a long walk into the country regale with gaufre and vin blanc or new milk and pain bis or pistolet au beurre roll and coffee
all this seem very pleasant and madame appear goodness itself and the teacher not so bad but they might be bad and the pupil perhaps a little noisy and rough but type of health and glee
thus do the view appear see through the enchantment of distance but there come a time when distance be to melt for i--when i be to be call down from my watch-tower of the nursery whence i have hitherto make my observation and be to be compel into close intercourse with this little world of the rue fossette
i be one day sit up-stair as usual hear the child their english lesson and at the same time turn a silk dress for madame when she come saunter into the room with that absorb air and brow of hard thought she sometimes wear and which make she look so little genial
drop into a seat opposite mine she remain some minute silent
desiree the eld girl be read to i some little essay of mrs
barbauld s and i be make her translate currently from english to french as she proceed by way of ascertain that she comprehend what she read madame listen
presently without preface or prelude she say almost in the tone of one make an accusation meess in england you be a governess no madame say i smile you be mistaken be this your first essay at teaching--this attempt with my child i assure she it be

one morning come on i abruptly and with the semblance of hurry she say she find herself place in a little dilemma

wilson the english master have fail to come at his hour she fear he be ill the pupil be wait in classe there be no one to give a lesson should i for once object to give a short dictation exercise just that the pupil might not have it to say they have miss their english lesson
in classe madame i ask
yes in classe in the second division where there be sixty pupil say i for i know the number and with my usual base habit of cowardice i shrink into my sloth like a snail into its shell and allege incapacity and impracticability as a pretext to escape action
if leave to myself i should infallibly have let this chance slip
inadventurous unstirred by impulse of practical ambition i be capable of sit twenty year teach infant the hornbook turn silk dress and make child s frock
not that true contentment dignify this infatuate resignation my work have neither charm for my taste nor hold on my interest but it seem to i a great thing to be without heavy anxiety and relieve from intimate trial the negation of severe suffering be the near approach to happiness i expect to know
besides i seem to hold two life--the life of thought and that of reality and provide the former be nourish with a sufficiency of the strange necromantic joy of fancy the privilege of the latter might remain limited to daily bread hourly work and a roof of shelter
come say madame as i stoop more busily than ever over the cutting-out of a child s pinafore leave that work but fifine want it madame fifine must want it then for i want you and as madame beck do really want and be resolve to have i--as she have long be dissatisfied with the english master with his shortcoming in punctuality and his careless method of tuition--as too she do not lack resolution and practical activity whether i lack they or not--she without more ado make i relinquish thimble and needle my hand be take into her and i be conduct down-stair
when we reach the carre a large square hall between the dwelling-house and the pensionnat she pause drop my hand face and scrutinize i
i be flushed and tremulous from head to foot tell it not in gath i believe i be cry
in fact the difficulty before i be far from be wholly imaginary some of they be real enough and not the least substantial lie in my want of mastery over the medium through which i should be oblige to teach
i have indeed study french closely since my arrival in villette learn its practice by day and its theory in every leisure moment at night to as late an hour as the rule of the house would allow candle-light but i be far from yet be able to trust my power of correct oral expression

i stand--not soothe nor win nor overwhelmed

there be three schoolroom all large

the first glance inform i that many of the pupil be more than girl--quite young woman i know that some of they be of noble family as nobility go in labassecour and i be well convince that not one amongst they be ignorant of my position in madame s household

the continental female be quite a different being to the insular female of the same age and class i never see such eye and brow in england

mesdemoiselles blanche virginie and angelique open the campaign by a series of tittering and whispering these soon swell into murmur and short laugh which the remoter bench catch up and echo more loudly
this grow revolt of sixty against one soon become oppressive enough my command of french be so limited and exercise under such cruel constraint

all i could now do be to walk up to blanche--mademoiselle de melcy a young baronne--the eld tallest handsome and most vicious--stand before her desk take from under her hand her exercise-book remount the estrade deliberately read the composition which i find very stupid and as deliberately and in the face of the whole school tear the blot page in two
this action avail to draw attention and check noise
one girl alone quite in the background persevere in the riot with undiminished energy

chapter ix
isidore
my time be now well and profitably fill up
what with teach other and study closely myself i have hardly a spare moment
it be pleasant
i feel i be get on not lie the stagnant prey of mould and rust but polish my faculty and whet they to a keen edge with constant use
experience of a certain kind lie before i on no narrow scale
villette be a cosmopolitan city and in this school be girl of almost every european nation and likewise of very varied rank in life
equality be much practise in labassecour though not republican in form it be nearly so in substance and at the desk of madame beck s establishment the young countess and the young bourgeoise sit side by side
nor could you always by outward indication decide which be noble and which plebeian except that indeed the latter have often frank and more courteous manner while the former bear away the bell for a delicately-balance combination of insolence and deceit
in the former there be often quick french blood mix with the marsh-phlegm i regret to say that the effect of this vivacious fluid chiefly appear in the oilier glibness with which flattery and fiction run from the tongue and in a manner light and livelier but quite heartless and insincere
to do all party justice the honest aboriginal labassecouriennes have an hypocrisy of their own too but it be of a coarse order such as could deceive few
whenever a lie be necessary for their occasion they bring it out with a careless ease and breadth altogether untrouble by the rebuke of conscience
not a soul in madame beck s house from the scullion to the directress herself but be above be ashamed of a lie they think nothing of it to invent might not be precisely a virtue but it be the most venial of fault
j ai menti plusieur fois form an item of every girl s and woman s monthly confession the priest hear unshocke and absolve unreluctant
if they have miss go to mass or read a chapter of a novel that be another thing these be crime whereof rebuke and penance be the unfailing weed
while yet but half-conscious of this state of thing and unlearn in its result i get on in my new sphere very well
after the first few difficult lesson give amidst peril and on the edge of a moral volcano that rumble under my foot and send spark and hot fume into my eye the eruptive spirit seem to subside as far as i be concern
my mind be a good deal bent on success i could not bear the thought of be baffle by mere undisciplined disaffection and wanton indocility in this first attempt to get on in life
many hour of the night i use to lie awake think what plan i have good adopt to get a reliable hold on these mutineer to bring this stiff-neck tribe under permanent influence
in the first place i see plainly that aid in no shape be to be expect from madame her righteous plan be to maintain an unbroken popularity with the pupil at any and every cost of justice or comfort to the teacher
for a teacher to seek her alliance in any crisis of insubordination be equivalent to secure her own expulsion
in intercourse with her pupil madame only take to herself what be pleasant amiable and recommendatory rigidly require of her lieutenant sufficiency for every annoying crisis where to act with adequate promptitude be to be unpopular
thus i must look only to myself
imprimis--it be clear as the day that this swinish multitude be not to be drive by force
they be to be humour bear with very patiently a courteous though sedate manner impress they a very rare flash of raillery do good
severe or continuous mental application they could not or would not bear heavy demand on the memory the reason the attention they reject point-blank
where an english girl of not more than average capacity and docility would quietly take a theme and bind herself to the task of comprehension and mastery a labassecourienne would laugh in your face and throw it back to you with the phrase dieu que c difficile
je n en veux pas
cela m ennuie trop a teacher who understand her business would take it back at once without hesitation contest or expostulation--proceed with even exaggerated care to smoothe every difficulty to reduce it to the level of their understanding return it to they thus modify and lie on the lash of sarcasm with unspare hand
they would feel the sting perhaps wince a little under it but they bear no malice against this sort of attack provide the sneer be not sour but hearty and that it hold well up to they in a clear light and bold type so that she who run might read their incapacity ignorance and sloth
they would riot for three additional line to a lesson but i never know they rebel against a wound give to their self-respect the little they have of that quality be train to be crush and it rather like the pressure of a firm heel than otherwise
by degree as i acquire fluency and freedom in their language and could make such application of its more nervous idiom as suit their case the eld and more intelligent girl begin rather to like i in their way i notice that whenever a pupil have be rouse to feel in her soul the stirring of worthy emulation or the quickening of honest shame from that date she be win
if i could but once make their usually large ear burn under their thick glossy hair all be comparatively well
by-and-by bouquet begin to be lay on my desk in the morning by way of acknowledgment for this little foreign attention i use sometimes to walk with a select few during recreation
in the course of conversation it befel once or twice that i make an unpremeditated attempt to rectify some of their singularly distorted notion of principle especially i express my idea of the evil and baseness of a lie
in an unguarded moment i chance to say that of the two error i consider falsehood bad than an occasional lapse in church-attendance
the poor girl be tutor to report in catholic ear whatever the protestant teacher say
an edify consequence ensue

behind the house at the rue fossette there be a garden--large consider that it lie in the heart of a city and to my recollection at this day it seem pleasant but time like distance lend to certain scene an influence so soften and where all be stone around blank wall and hot pavement how precious seem one shrub how lovely an enclosed and plant spot of ground

that in year go by--how long go by i cannot tell but i think some century--before the city have over-spread this quarter and when it be till ground and avenue and such deep and leafy seclusion as ought to embosom a religious house-that something have happen on this site which rouse fear and inflict horror have leave to the place the inheritance of a ghost-story
a vague tale go of a black and white nun sometimes on some night or night of the year see in some part of this vicinage
the ghost must have be build out some age ago for there be house all round now but certain convent-relic in the shape of old and huge fruit-tree yet consecrate the spot and at the foot of one--a methuselah of a pear- tree dead all but a few bough which still faithfully renew their perfume snow in spring and their honey-sweet pendant in autumn--you see in scrap away the mossy earth between the half-bar root a glimpse of slab smooth hard and black
the legend go unconfirmed and unaccredite but still propagate that this be the portal of a vault imprison deep beneath that ground on whose surface grass grow and flower bloom the bone of a girl whom a monkish conclave of the drear middle age have here bury alive for some sin against her vow
her shadow it be that trembler have fear through long generation after her poor frame be dust her black robe and white veil that for timid eye moonlight and shade have mock as they fluctuate in the night-wind through the garden-thicket
independently of romantic rubbish however that old garden have its charm
on summer morning i use to rise early to enjoy they alone on summer evening to linger solitary to keep tryste with the rise moon or taste one kiss of the evening breeze or fancy rather than feel the freshness of dew descend
the turf be verdant the gravelled walk be white sun-bright nasturtium cluster beautiful about the root of the dodder orchard giant
there be a large berceau above which spread the shade of an acacia there be a small more sequester bower nestle in the vine which run all along a high and grey wall and gather their tendril in a knot of beauty and hang their cluster in love profusion about the favoured spot where jasmine and ivy meet and marry they
doubtless at high noon in the broad vulgar middle of the day when madame beck s large school turn out rampant and externe and pensionnaire be spread abroad vie with the denizen of the boy college close at hand in the brazen exercise of their lung and limb--doubtless then the garden be a trite trodden-down place enough
but at sunset or the hour of salut when the externe be go home and the boarder quiet at their study pleasant be it then to stray down the peaceful alley and hear the bell of st
jean baptiste peal out with their sweet soft exalted sound
i be walk thus one evening and have be detain far within the verge of twilight than usual by the still-deepen calm the mellow coolness the fragrant breathing with which flower no sunshine could win now answer the persuasion of the dew
i see by a light in the oratory window that the catholic household be then gather to evening prayer--a rite from attendance on which i now and then as a protestant exempt myself
one moment long whisper solitude and the summer moon stay with we all be truly quiet now for another quarter of an hour your presence will not be miss the day s heat and bustle have tire you enjoy these precious minute the windowless back of house build in this garden and in particular the whole of one side be skirt by the rear of a long line of premise--be the boarding-house of the neighbouring college
this rear however be all blank stone with the exception of certain attic loophole high up open from the sleeping-room of the women- servant and also one casement in a low story say to mark the chamber or study of a master
but though thus secure an alley which run parallel with the very high wall on that side the garden be forbid to be enter by the pupil
it be call indeed l allee defendue and any girl set foot there would have render herself liable to as severe a penalty as the mild rule of madame beck s establishment permit
teacher might indeed go there with impunity but as the walk be narrow and the neglect shrub be grow very thick and close on each side weave overhead a roof of branch and leaf which the sun s ray penetrate but in rare chequer this alley be seldom enter even during day and after dusk be carefully shun
from the first i be tempt to make an exception to this rule of avoidance the seclusion the very gloom of the walk attract i
for a long time the fear of seem singular scar i away but by degree as people become accustomed to i and my habit and to such shade of peculiarity as be engraine in my nature--shade certainly not strike enough to interest and perhaps not prominent enough to offend but bear in and with i and no more to be part with than my identity--by slow degree i become a frequenter of this strait and narrow path
i make myself gardener of some tintless flower that grow between its closely-rank shrub i clear away the relic of past autumn choke up a rustic seat at the far end
borrowing of goton the cuisiniere a pail of water and a scrubbing- brush i make this seat clean
madame see i at work and smile approbation whether sincerely or not i donot know but she seem sincere

on the night in question i be sit on the hidden seat reclaim from fungi and mould listen to what seem the far-off sound of the city
far off in truth they be not this school be in the city s centre hence it be but five minute walk to the park scarce ten to building of palatial splendour
quite near be wide street brightly light teem at this moment with life carriage be roll through they to ball or to the opera
the same hour which toll curfew for our convent which extinguish each lamp and drop the curtain round each couch rang for the gay city about we the summon to festal enjoyment
of this contrast i think not however gay instinct my nature have few ball or opera i have never see and though often i have hear they describe and even wish to see they it be not the wish of one who hop to partake a pleasure if she could only reach it--who feel fit to shine in some bright distant sphere could she but thither win her way it be no yearning to attain no hunger to taste only the calm desire to look on a new thing
a moon be in the sky not a full moon but a young crescent
i see she through a space in the bough overhead
she and the star visible beside she be no stranger where all else be strange my childhood know they
i have see that golden sign with the dark globe in its curve lean back on azure beside an old thorn at the top of an old field in old england in long past day just as it now lean back beside a stately spire in this continental capital
oh my childhood
i have feeling passive as i live little as i speak cold as i look when i think of past day i could feel
about the present it be well to be stoical about the future--such a future as mine--to be dead
and in catalepsy and a dead trance i studiously hold the quick of my nature
at that time i well remember whatever could excite--certain accident of the weather for instance be almost dread by i because they wake the being i be always lulling and stir up a craving cry i could not satisfy
one night a thunder-storm break a sort of hurricane shake we in our bed the catholics rise in panic and pray to their saint
as for i the temp take hold of i with tyranny i be roughly rouse and oblige to live
i get up and dress myself and creep outside the casement close by my bed sit on its ledge with my foot on the roof of a lower adjoining building
it be wet it be wild it be pitch-dark
within the dormitory they gather round the night-lamp in consternation pray loud
i could not go in too resistless be the delight of stay with the wild hour black and full of thunder peal out such an ode as language never deliver to man--too terribly glorious the spectacle of cloud split and pierce by white and blind bolt
i do long achingly then and for four and twenty hour afterwards for something to fetch i out of my present existence and lead i upwards and onwards
this longing and all of a similar kind it be necessary to knock on the head which i do figuratively after the manner of jael to sisera drive a nail through their temple
unlike sisera they do not die they be but transiently stun and at interval would turn on the nail with a rebellious wrench then do the temple bleed and the brain thrill to its core
to-night i be not so mutinous nor so miserable
my sisera lie quiet in the tent slumbering and if his pain ache through his slumber something like an angel--the ideal--kneel near drop balm on the soothe temple hold before the sealed eye a magic glass of which the sweet solemn vision be repeat in dream and shed a reflex from her moonlight wing and robe over the transfix sleeper over the tent threshold over all the landscape lie without
jael the stern woman sit apart relent somewhat over her captive but more prone to dwell on the faithful expectation of heber come home
by which word i mean that the cool peace and dewy sweetness of the night fill i with a mood of hope not hope on any definite point but a general sense of encouragement and heart-ease

alas no good come of it
i presently the rude real burst coarsely in--all evil grovel and repellent as she too often be

the scene of the etude du soir be always the refectory a much small apartment than any of the three class or schoolroom for here none save the boarder be ever admit and these number only a score
two lamp hang from the ceiling over the two table these be light at dusk and their kindling be the signal for school- book be set aside a grave demeanour assume general silence enforce and then commence la lecture pieuse this say lecture pieuse be i soon find mainly design as a wholesome mortification of the intellect a useful humiliation of the reason and such a dose for common sense as she might digest at her leisure and thrive on as she good could
the book bring out it be never change but when finish recommence be a venerable volume old as the hill--grey as the hotel de ville
i would have give two franc for the chance of get that book once into my band turn over the sacred yellow leaf ascertain the title and peruse with my own eye the enormous figment which as an unworthy heretic it be only permit i to drink in with my bewildered ear
this book contain legend of the saint
good god
i speak the word reverently what legend they be
what gasconade rascal those saint must have be if they first boast these exploit or invent these miracle

the ear burn on each side of my head as i listen perforce to tale of moral martyrdom inflict by rome the dread boast of confessor who have wickedly abuse their office trample to deep degradation high-bear lady making of countess and princesse the most tormented slave under the sun
story like that of conrad and elizabeth of hungary recur again and again with all its dreadful viciousness sicken tyranny and black impiety tale that be nightmare of oppression privation and agony
i sit out this lecture pieuse for some night as well as i could and as quietly too only once break off the point of my scissor by involuntarily stick they somewhat deep in the worm-eat board of the table before i

no mause headrigg ever feel a strong call to take up her testimony against sergeant bothwell than i--to speak my mind in this matter of the popish lecture pieuse however i do manage somehow to curb and rein in and though always as soon as rosine come to light the lamp i shoot from the room quickly yet also i do it quietly seize that vantage moment give by the little bustle before the dead silence and vanishing whilst the boarder put their book away

in winter i seek the long class and pace they fast to keep myself warm--fortunate if the moon shine and if there be only star soon reconcile to their dim gleam or even to the total eclipse of their absence
in summer it be never quite dark and then i go up-stair to my own quarter of the long dormitory open my own casement that chamber be light by five casement large as great door and lean out look forth upon the city beyond the garden and listen to band- music from the park or the palace-square think meantime my own thought live my own life in my own still shadow-world

she have a wonderful passion for present and in this point the third teacher--a person otherwise characterless and insignificant--closely resemble she
this last-name have also one other distinctive property--that of avarice
in she reign the love of money for its own sake
the sight of a piece of gold would bring into her eye a green glisten singular to witness

chapter xv
the long vacation
follow madame beck s fete with its three precede week of relaxation its brief twelve hour burst of hilarity and dissipation and its one subsequent day of utter languor come a period of reaction two month of real application of close hard study
these two month be the last of the annee scolaire be indeed the only genuine work month in the year
to they be procrastinated-- into they concentrate alike by professor mistress and pupils-- the main burden of preparation for the examination precede the distribution of prize
candidate for reward have then to work in good earnest master and teacher have to set their shoulder to the wheel to urge on the backward and diligently aid and train the more promising
a showy demonstration--a telling exhibition--must be get up for public view and all mean be fair to this end
i scarcely note how the other teacher go to work i have my own business to mind and my task be not the least onerous be to imbue some ninety set of brain with a due tincture of what they consider a most complicated and difficult science that of the english language and to drill ninety tongue in what for they be an almost impossible pronunciation--the lisping and hiss dental of the isles
the examination-day arrive
awful day
prepare for with anxious care dress for with silent despatch--nothing vaporous or fluttering now--no white gauze or azure streamer the grave close compact be the order of the toilette
it seem to i that i be this day especially doom--the main burden and trial fall on i alone of all the female teacher

de bassompierre
those who live in retirement whose life have fall amid the seclusion of school or of other wall-in and guard dwelling be liable to be suddenly and for a long while drop out of the memory of their friend the denizen of a free world
unaccountably perhaps and close upon some space of unusually frequent intercourse-- some congerie of rather exciting little circumstance whose natural sequel would rather seem to be the quickening than the suspension of communication--there fall a stilly pause a wordless silence a long blank of oblivion
unbroken always be this blank alike entire and unexplained
the letter the message once frequent be cut off the visit formerly periodical cease to occur the book paper or other token that indicate remembrance come no more
always there be excellent reason for these lapse if the hermit but know they
though he be stagnant in his cell his connection without be whirl in the very vortex of life
that void interval which pass for he so slowly that the very clock seem at a stand and the wingless hour plod by in the likeness of tired tramp prone to rest at milestone--that same interval perhaps teem with event and pant with hurry for his friend
the hermit--if he be a sensible hermit--will swallow his own thought and lock up his own emotion during these week of inward winter
he will know that destiny design he to imitate on occasion the dormouse and he will be conformable make a tidy ball of himself creep into a hole of life s wall and submit decently to the drift which blow in and soon block he up preserve he in ice for the season
let he say it be quite right it ought to be so since so it be and perhaps one day his snow-sepulchre will open spring s softness will return the sun and south-wind will reach he the bud of hedge and carolling of bird and singing of liberated stream will call he to kindly resurrection
perhaps this may be the case perhaps not the frost may get into his heart and never thaw more when spring come a crow or a pie may pick out of the wall only his dormouse-bone
well even in that case all will be right it be to be suppose he know from the first he be mortal and must one day go the way of all flesh as well soon as syne follow that eventful evening at the theatre come for i seven week as bare as seven sheet of blank paper no word be write on one of they not a visit not a token

the mid-blank be always a becloud point for the solitary his nerve ache with the strain of long expectancy the doubt hitherto repel gather now to a mass and--strong in accumulation--roll back upon he with a force which savour of vindictiveness

of course i do not blame myself for suffering i thank god i have a truer sense of justice than to fall into any imbecile extravagance of self-accusation and as to blame other for silence in my reason i well know they blameless and in my heart acknowledge they so but it be a rough and heavy road to travel and i long for well day

that church whose dark half-ruinous turret overlook the square be the venerable and formerly opulent shrine of the magi
but wealth and greatness have long since stretch their gild pinion and flee hence leave these their ancient nest perhaps to house penury for a time or perhaps to stand cold and empty moulder untenante in the course of winter
as i cross this deserted place on whose pavement drop almost as large as a five-franc piece be now slowly darken i see in its whole expanse no symptom or evidence of life except what be give in the figure of an infirm old priest who go past bend and prop on a staff--the type of eld and decay
he have issue from the very house to which i be direct and when i pause before the door just close after he and ring the bell he turn to look at i
nor do he soon avert his gaze perhaps he think i with my basket of summer fruit and my lack of the dignity age confer an incongruous figure in such a scene
i know have a young ruddy-face bonne open the door to admit i i should have think such a one little in harmony with her dwelling but when i find myself confront by a very old woman wear a very antique peasant costume a cap alike hideous and costly with long flap of native lace a petticoat and jacket of cloth and sabot more like little boat than shoe it seem all right and soothingly in character
the expression of her face be not quite so soothing as the cut of her costume anything more cantankerous i have seldom see she would scarcely reply to my inquiry after madame walravens i believe she would have snatch the basket of fruit from my hand have not the old priest hobble up check she and himself lend an ear to the message with which i be charge
his apparent deafness render it a little difficult to make he fully understand that i must see madame walravens and consign the fruit into her own hand
at last however he comprehend the fact that such be my order and that duty enjoin their literal fulfilment

the room be large and have a fine old ceiling and almost church-like window of colour-glass but it be desolate and in the shadow of a come storm look strangely lower
within--open a small room there however the blind of the single casement be close through the deep gloom few detail of furniture be apparent
these few i amuse myself by puzzle to make out and in particular i be attract by the outline of a picture on the wall
by-and-by the picture seem to give way to my bewilderment it shake it sink it roll back into nothing its vanishing leave an opening arched lead into an arched passage with a mystic winding stair both passage and stair be of cold stone uncarpeted and unpainted
down this donjon stair descend a tap tap like a stick soon there fall on the step a shadow and last of all i be aware of a substance
yet be it actual substance this appearance approach i
this obstruction partially darken the arch
it draw near and i see it well

well might this old square be name quarter of the magi--well might the three tower overlook it own for godfather three mystic sage of a dead and dark art

distincter even than these scenic detail stand the chief figure--cunegonde the sorceress
malevola the evil fairy
how be she
she might be three foot high but she have no shape her skinny hand rest upon each other and press the gold knob of a wand-like ivory staff
her face be large set not upon her shoulder but before her breast she seem to have no neck i should have say there be a hundred year in her feature and more perhaps in her eye--her malign unfriendly eye with thick grey brow above and livid lid all round
how severely they view i with a sort of dull displeasure

but her chief point be her jewel she have long clear earring blaze with a lustre which could not be borrow or false she have ring on her skeleton hand with thick gold hoop and stone--purple green and blood-red

que i voulez-vous say she hoarsely with the voice rather of male than of female old age and indeed a silver beard bristle she chin

be my dearest first on earth we walk back to the rue fossette by moonlight--such moonlight as fall on eden--shin through the shade of the great garden and haply gild a path glorious for a step divine--a presence nameless
once in their life some man and woman go back to these first fresh day of our great sire and mother--taste that grand morning s dew-- bathe in its sunrise

paul s consent she have be affiance for month to one heinrich muehler a wealthy young german merchant and be to be marry in the course of a year

jean baptiste s clock toll nine
at this hour in this house eighteen month since have this man at my side bent before i look into my face and eye and arbitere my destiny
this very evening he have again stoop gaze and decreed
how different the look--how far otherwise the fate
he deem i bear under his star he seem to have spread over i its beam like a banner
